function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Assume Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA declined 25% while AI capex remained at Q1 2026 annualized levels. Quantify the reduction in internal funding capacity and assess the implications for financing flexibility.
I'll analyze this question by first retrieving the SpaceX S-1 filing to find the Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA and AI capex figures.
steps.reasoning_content:
The user is asking about SpaceX's S-1 filing, specifically about the Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA and AI capex. I need to:

1. Find the Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA from the S-1 filing
2. Find the AI capex (likely from xAI segment) for Q1 2026
3. Calculate the annualized AI capex
4. Calculate the 25% decline in Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA
5. Quantify the reduction in internal funding capacity
6. Assess implications for financing flexibility

Let me start by parsing the SpaceX S-1 filing.

# AI Segment Capital Expenditures

Based on the capital expenditures by segment table and supporting narrative in the document:

| Period | AI Segment Capex |
|---|---|
| **Q1 2026** (Three Months Ended March 31, 2026) | **$7,723 million** |
| **Full Year 2025** (Year Ended December 31, 2025) | **$12,727 million** |

## Supporting Context

The document further explains the Q1 2026 figure:

> "AI capital expenditures for the three months ended March 31, 2026 increased **$5,156 million** to **$7,723 million** compared to **$2,567 million** for the three months ended March 31, 2025. The increase was primarily driven by investments in the rapid expansion of our terrestrial data centers, including the development, construction, and equipping of new facilities and supporting infrastructure."

## Full Segment Capex Breakdown for Reference

**Q1 2026:**
- Space: $1,052 million
- Connectivity: $1,332 million
- AI: $7,723 million
- **Total: $10,107 million**

**Full Year 2025:**
- Space: $3,832 million
- Connectivity: $4,178 million
- AI: $12,727 million
- **Total: $20,737 million**

The AI segment represented approximately **76%** of total capex in Q1 2026 and **61%** for full year 2025, reflecting the company's aggressive investment in data center infrastructure following the xAI acquisition in February 2026.

steps.message: I have the key figures. Let me also retrieve additional context on the company's overall cash flow, financing structure, and any discussion of internal funding capacity.
steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have the key figures:

**Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA:**
- Q1 2026: $2,087 million
- Full Year 2025: $7,168 million

**AI Segment Capex:**
- Q1 2026: $7,723 million
- Full Year 2025: $12,727 million

Now I need to:
1. Calculate the 25% decline in Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA
2. Calculate the annualized AI capex from Q1 2026 levels
3. Quantify the reduction in internal funding capacity
4. Assess implications for financing flexibility

Let me think about this:

**Step 1: Annualize Q1 2026 Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA**
- Q1 2026: $2,087 million
- Annualized: $2,087 × 4 = $8,348 million

**Step 2: Apply 25% decline**
- Declined annualized: $8,348 × 0.75 = $6,261 million
- Reduction: $8,348 × 0.25 = $2,087 million

Wait, let me think about this more carefully. The question says "Assume Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA declined 25%". This could mean:
- 25% decline from Q1 2026 annualized level
- 25% decline from full year 2025 level

Let me consider both interpretations. The most natural reading is that the decline is from the current run-rate (Q1 2026 annualized), since the question pairs it with "AI capex remained at Q1 2026 annualized levels."

So:
- Q1 2026 Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA annualized: $2,087 × 4 = $8,348 million
- 25% decline: $8,348 × 0.25 = $2,087 million reduction
- New annualized Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA: $8,348 × 0.75 = $6,261 million

**Step 3: Annualize Q1 2026 AI capex**
- Q1 2026 AI capex: $7,723 million
- Annualized: $7,723 × 4 = $30,892 million

**Step 4: Calculate internal funding capacity**

Internal funding capacity = Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA - AI capex (annualized)

Before decline:
- $8,348 - $30,892 = -$22,544 million (deficit)

After 25% decline:
- $6,261 - $30,892 = -$24,631 million (deficit)

Reduction in internal funding capacity:
- $6,261 - $8,348 = -$2,087 million

So the internal funding capacity declines by $2,087 million (or $2.087 billion).

Wait, I should also consider the other segments. Let me think about this more carefully.

The question is about "internal funding capacity" - this typically refers to the cash flow generated by the business that can be used to fund capex. The Connectivity segment is the cash-generative segment, while the AI segment is the cash-consuming segment.

Let me also check the Space segment and other relevant figures. Let me also look at the broader context of the filing to understand the financing flexibility discussion.

Actually, let me also consider whether the question is asking about the entire company's internal funding capacity or just the Connectivity segment's contribution to it.

The question says "Quantify the reduction in internal funding capacity" - this suggests we need to look at the overall internal funding capacity, which would be the cash generated by the business (primarily from Connectivity, since Space and AI are loss-making) minus the capex requirements.

Let me retrieve more information about the company's overall cash flow and financing structure.

arguments.prompt: What is the total Segment Adjusted EBITDA for all segments (Space, Connectivity, AI) for Q1 2026 and full year 2025 in {{spacex_s1}}? Also, what is the total capital expenditure for the company?

In {{spacex_s1}}, what does the filing say about financing flexibility, liquidity, cash position, debt, and capital requirements? Please provide details on cash and cash equivalents, total debt, and any discussion of funding needs.

results.content:
# Total Segment Adjusted EBITDA

## Q1 2026 (Three Months Ended March 31, 2026)

| Segment | Segment Adjusted EBITDA (in millions) |
|---|---|
| Space | $(351) |
| Connectivity | $2,087 |
| AI | $(609) |
| **Total Reportable Segments** | **$1,127** |

## Full Year 2025 (Year Ended December 31, 2025)

| Segment | Segment Adjusted EBITDA (in millions) |
|---|---|
| Space | $653 |
| Connectivity | $7,168 |
| AI | $(1,237) |
| **Total Reportable Segments** | **$6,584** |

---

# Total Capital Expenditures

| Period | Total Capital Expenditures (in millions) |
|---|---|
| Q1 2026 | $10,107 |
| Full Year 2025 | $20,737 |

**Key observations:**
- The **Connectivity segment** (driven by Starlink) is the primary contributor to positive Segment Adjusted EBITDA in both periods.
- The **AI segment** is in an investment-heavy, early-stage phase, generating negative Segment Adjusted EBITDA of $(609) million in Q1 2026 and $(1,237) million in 2025.
- The **Space segment** turned negative in Q1 2026 ($(351) million) compared to positive $653 million in full year 2025, driven by accelerated Starship development investments.
- Capital expenditures surged dramatically, particularly in the AI segment, which alone accounted for $7,723 million in Q1 2026 (vs. $2,567 million in Q1 2025).

Based on the provided filing chunks, here are the details regarding the company's financing flexibility, liquidity, cash position, debt, and capital requirements:

**Liquidity and Cash Position**
*   **Primary Sources of Liquidity:** The company's primary sources of liquidity are cash flows generated from operations, cash and cash equivalents, short-term marketable securities, and borrowings under credit facilities. 
*   **Cash and Cash Equivalents:** As of March 31, 2026, the company had **$15,852 million** in cash and cash equivalents. Including restricted cash ($67 million in prepaid expenses and $689 million in other assets), the total cash and restricted cash balance was **$16,608 million**. 
*   **Marketable Securities:** The company held **$7,823 million** in short-term marketable securities as of March 31, 2026. These consist primarily of U.S. Government debt securities, time deposits, and certificates of deposit.
*   **Investment Policy:** The company's investment policy is focused on the preservation of capital and supporting liquidity requirements. A hypothetical 100 basis point change in interest rates would have an immaterial impact on interest income.

**Debt Obligations**
*   **Total Debt:** As of March 31, 2026, the company had **$29,132 million** in aggregate principal amount of indebtedness outstanding. The total long-term debt on the balance sheet (net of unamortized deferred financing costs) was **$29,111 million**.
*   **Debt Composition:** The debt primarily consists of a **$20,000 million** SpaceX Bridge Loan, **$9,105 million** in "Other Financings," and **$27 million** in X 2027 and X 2030 Notes. 
*   **SpaceX Credit Facility:** The company has a five-year senior unsecured revolving credit agreement allowing borrowings up to **$1,500 million**. As of March 31, 2026, no amounts were outstanding. It includes sublimits of up to $150 million for financial letters of credit and $1,000 million for performance letters of credit. It is subject to a maximum financial covenant requiring a Consolidated Leverage Ratio of no greater than 3.75 to 1.0 (with temporary increases to 4.25 to 1.0 following qualified acquisitions). 
*   **Maturity Profile:** No debt principal payments are due until August 28, 2027, if the company chooses not to extend. The SpaceX Credit Facility terminates on February 7, 2030.
*   **Lease Obligations:** Total minimum lease payments were **$5,823 million**, of which $1,026 million is due within the current fiscal year.
*   **Risks of Indebtedness:** The filing notes that this significant level of indebtedness could adversely affect the business by increasing vulnerability to adverse economic conditions, requiring a substantial portion of cash flow for debt payments, limiting flexibility, and exposing the company to variable interest rate risks.

**Financing Flexibility and Capital Requirements**
*   **Capital Allocation:** Cash generated from core operations is used to fund R&D projects (like the Starship rocket and next-generation satellites), construct future data centers, and continue expanding AI-enabled products.
*   **Flexibility to Adjust Spending:** The company retains flexibility to adjust spending across segments. If near-term data center needs decrease or ramp more slowly than expected, it can reduce capital expenditures in that segment and reallocate funds to other business priorities.
*   **Raising Additional Capital:** The company continually evaluates its cash needs and may raise additional capital or seek alternative financing sources to fund rapid growth. This includes drawdowns on existing or new debt facilities, notes offerings, or bank borrowings. The company may also seek to refinance the SpaceX Bridge Loan or voluntarily repay certain indebtedness early.
*   **Funding Sufficiency:** The company believes it has sufficient sources of funding to meet its business requirements for at least the next twelve months from the issuance of the consolidated financial statements.

**Cash Flow from Financing Activities**
*   **Recent Financing Activities:** Net cash provided by financing activities was **$7,125 million** for the three months ended March 31, 2026, and **$26,350 million** for the year ended December 31, 2025. 
*   **Drivers:** The recent increase in financing cash flows was primarily driven by proceeds from the SpaceX Bridge Loan and other AI segment financing arrangements ($17,950 million in Q1 2026; $16,055 million in FY 2025), as well as proceeds from the sale of capital stock ($7,420 million in Q1 2026). These inflows were partially offset by payments on existing debt obligations, debt extinguishment costs, and repurchases of capital stock.

steps.message: Let me also retrieve the cash flow from operations and any additional context on the funding gap.
steps.reasoning_content: Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me also check the cash flow from operations to understand the internal funding capacity better.

arguments.prompt: In {{spacex_s1}}, what is the cash flow from operations for Q1 2026 and full year 2025? Also, what is the free cash flow or any discussion of the gap between operating cash flow and capital expenditures?
In {{spacex_s1}}, what does the filing say about the SpaceX Bridge Loan, its terms, maturity, and any covenants? Also, what is the Consolidated Leverage Ratio and how is it calculated?

results.content:
# Cash Flow from Operations

## Operating Cash Flow Figures

| Period | Net Cash Provided by Operating Activities |
|---|---|
| **Q1 2026** (three months ended March 31, 2026) | **$1,047 million** |
| **Full Year 2025** (year ended December 31, 2025) | **$6,785 million** |

For context, Q1 2025 operating cash flow was $727 million, and FY2024 was $5,776 million — so operating cash flow is growing year-over-year.

## Drivers of Operating Cash Flow (per MD&A)

**Q1 2026 vs. Q1 2025 (+$320 million increase):**
- Primarily driven by a **$1,153 million increase in deferred revenue** from upfront payments by Space and Connectivity customers
- Partially offset by lower net income exclusive of non-cash items

**FY2025 vs. FY2024 (+$1,009 million increase):**
- Higher net income exclusive of non-cash items
- $1,080 million increase in accounts payable and other liabilities (infrastructure expansion and payment timing)
- Higher deferred revenue from aviation customer upfront payments
- Partially offset by a $449 million increase in accounts receivable, prepaid expenses, and inventory

## Free Cash Flow / Gap Between Operating Cash Flow and CapEx

The filing does **not** explicitly use the term "free cash flow," but the gap between operating cash flow and capital expenditures is stark and clearly disclosed:

| Period | Operating Cash Flow | Capital Expenditures | Implied "Free Cash Flow" |
|---|---|---|---|
| Q1 2026 | $1,047M | $10,107M | **$(9,060)M** |
| FY 2025 | $6,785M | $20,737M | **$(13,952)M** |
| FY 2024 | $5,776M | $11,163M | $(5,387)M |
| FY 2023 | $4,520M | $4,415M | $105M |

### Key Observations on the CapEx Gap

1. **CapEx is scaling dramatically faster than operating cash flow.** In Q1 2026 alone, capex ($10.1B) was nearly equal to all of FY2024 ($11.2B) and roughly half of FY2025 ($20.7B).

2. **AI segment is the dominant driver.** The segment-level capex breakdown shows:
   - Q1 2026 AI capex: **$7,723 million** (76% of total)
   - FY2025 AI capex: **$12,727 million** (61% of total)
   - By contrast, Space capex was $1,052M (Q1 2026) / $3,832M (FY2025) and Connectivity was $1,332M / $4,178M.

3. **The gap is financed externally.** With operating cash flow far short of capex, the company has relied heavily on financing activities:
   - Q1 2026 financing: $7,125M
   - FY2025 financing: $26,350M
   - FY2024 financing: $11,830M

4. **Noncash capex also significant.** Supplemental disclosures show additional property, plant, and equipment purchases included in accrued expenses and accounts payable of **$10,649M (Q1 2026)** and **$7,088M (FY2025)**, plus PP&E financed by other financings of $2,684M in Q1 2026 — meaning the total capital investment commitment is even larger than the cash capex figure suggests.

5. **Debt load backing this investment.** As of March 31, 2026, the company had **$29,132 million** in aggregate principal amount of indebtedness outstanding, with no principal payments due until August 2027 (if not extended).

### Summary

The company is in a heavy investment phase — particularly in AI infrastructure — where operating cash flow, while growing, is dwarfed by capital expenditures. The resulting funding gap (roughly $9B in Q1 2026 alone and ~$14B for FY2025) is being bridged through debt and equity financing rather than internally generated cash, which is typical of an early-stage, capital-intensive growth trajectory but represents a significant cash burn profile.

Based on the provided filing, here are the details regarding the SpaceX Bridge Loan, its terms, maturity, covenants, and the Consolidated Leverage Ratio:

**Overview of the SpaceX Bridge Loan**
In March 2026, SpaceX entered into a new unsecured bridge term loan credit agreement providing for an aggregate principal amount of $20,000 million with a syndicate of lenders, with Goldman Sachs Bank USA acting as the administrative agent. The proceeds from this loan were used to extinguish and pay off legacy debts, specifically the X B-1 Term Loan, X B-3 Term Loan, xAI Fixed Rate Loan, xAI Floating Rate Loan, and the xAI 12.5% Senior Secured Notes. The remaining proceeds were used for general corporate purposes. 

**Maturity**
The SpaceX Bridge Loan matures on September 2, 2027. The Company has the option to extend the maturity date twice, each time by three months (for a total of six months), reaching a final extended maturity date of March 2, 2028. These extensions are subject to the absence of a continuing default and the payment of an extension fee of 0.25% of the aggregate outstanding principal per extension.

**Terms (Interest Rates, Fees, and Repayment)**
*   **Interest Rates:** At the Company’s election, interest is charged at a rate per annum equal to either:
    *   (i) Term SOFR plus an applicable margin ranging from 0.75% to 1.75% (depending on the Company's debt rating), or 
    *   (ii) A base rate equal to the highest of (a) the Federal Funds Rate plus 0.5%, (b) the Prime Rate, (c) Term SOFR plus 1.0%, and (d) 1.0%, plus an applicable margin ranging from 0.00% to 0.75%.
*   **Duration Fees:** The Company must pay duration fees equal to 0.125% of the outstanding principal on the first anniversary of closing, and 0.25% of the outstanding principal on the fifteen-month anniversary of closing.
*   **Effective Interest Rate:** As of March 31, 2026, the effective interest rate on outstanding borrowings was 4.58%.
*   **Prepayment:** The loan may be prepaid at any time, in whole or in part, without premium or penalty.
*   **Mandatory Repayment:** The Company is required to use an amount equal to the net cash proceeds of certain debt financings to repay amounts outstanding under the Bridge Loan. Additionally, the Company must apply an amount equal to the net proceeds of a qualified initial public offering (IPO) to repay such amounts within six months following receipt of those proceeds.
*   **Guarantors:** The obligations under the SpaceX Bridge Loan are guaranteed on a joint and several basis by X Corp., X.AI LLC, and CTC Property LLC (each a subsidiary of the Company).

**Covenants**
The SpaceX Bridge Loan contains customary events of default and affirmative and negative covenants. These include restrictions on:
*   Liens
*   Subsidiary indebtedness
*   Fundamental changes (including an explicit prohibition on the disposition of Starlink assets and other material businesses outside the consolidated group)
*   Changes in the nature of the Company’s business

The sole financial maintenance covenant is the requirement to maintain the Consolidated Leverage Ratio. As of March 31, 2026, the Company was in compliance with all covenants under the loan.

**Consolidated Leverage Ratio and Calculation**
The Consolidated Leverage Ratio is the sole financial maintenance covenant required by the SpaceX Bridge Loan. 

*   **Calculation:** It is defined as the ratio of consolidated funded indebtedness (net of 85% of unrestricted cash) to Consolidated EBITDA (as defined in the loan agreement).
*   **Requirement:** The Company is required to maintain a Consolidated Leverage Ratio of no greater than **3.75 to 1.0** as of the end of each fiscal quarter.
*   **Step-up Provision:** There is a temporary step-up allowed to **4.25 to 1.0** for a period of four fiscal quarters following a qualifying acquisition of at least $1.0 billion.

steps.message: Now I have all the key figures. Let me perform the calculations to quantify the reduction in internal funding capacity.
steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have all the information I need. Let me organize the analysis:

**Key Figures:**

1. **Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA:**
   - Q1 2026: $2,087 million
   - Annualized Q1 2026: $2,087 × 4 = $8,348 million
   - Full Year 2025: $7,168 million

2. **AI Segment Capex:**
   - Q1 2026: $7,723 million
   - Annualized Q1 2026: $7,723 × 4 = $30,892 million
   - Full Year 2025: $12,727 million

3. **Total Segment Adjusted EBITDA:**
   - Q1 2026: $1,127 million
   - Annualized Q1 2026: $1,127 × 4 = $4,508 million
   - Full Year 2025: $6,584 million

4. **Total Capex:**
   - Q1 2026: $10,107 million
   - Annualized Q1 2026: $10,107 × 4 = $40,428 million
   - Full Year 2025: $20,737 million

5. **Cash Position (as of March 31, 2026):**
   - Cash and cash equivalents: $15,852 million
   - Short-term marketable securities: $7,823 million
   - Total liquidity: $23,675 million

6. **Debt:**
   - Total debt: $29,132 million
   - SpaceX Bridge Loan: $20,000 million
   - Other Financings: $9,105 million

7. **Consolidated Leverage Ratio covenant:** ≤ 3.75x (or 4.25x temporarily after qualifying acquisition)

**Analysis:**

The question asks to:
1. Assume Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA declined 25%
2. AI capex remained at Q1 2026 annualized levels
3. Quantify the reduction in internal funding capacity
4. Assess implications for financing flexibility

**Step 1: Annualize Q1 2026 figures**

Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA (annualized Q1 2026):
$2,087M × 4 = $8,348M

AI Capex (annualized Q1 2026):
$7,723M × 4 = $30,892M

**Step 2: Apply 25% decline to Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA**

Reduction: $8,348M × 25% = $2,087M
New Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA: $8,348M × 75% = $6,261M

**Step 3: Calculate internal funding capacity**

The "internal funding capacity" can be interpreted as the cash generated internally that can fund capex. The Connectivity segment is the primary cash-generative segment. 

Internal funding capacity = Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA - AI Capex (annualized)

Before decline:
$8,348M - $30,892M = -$22,544M (deficit)

After 25% decline:
$6,261M - $30,892M = -$24,631M (deficit)

Reduction in internal funding capacity:
$6,261M - $8,348M = -$2,087M

So the internal funding capacity declines by $2,087 million (or $2.087 billion).

Alternatively, we could look at the total internal funding capacity (all segments):

Total Segment Adjusted EBITDA (annualized Q1 2026):
$1,127M × 4 = $4,508M

After 25% decline in Connectivity:
New Total Segment Adjusted EBITDA = $4,508M - $2,087M = $2,421M

Total Capex (annualized Q1 2026):
$10,107M × 4 = $40,428M

Internal funding capacity (all segments):
Before: $4,508M - $40,428M = -$35,920M
After: $2,421M - $40,428M = -$38,007M
Reduction: -$2,087M

The reduction is the same $2,087M because only the Connectivity segment changes.

**Step 4: Assess implications for financing flexibility**

The reduction in internal funding capacity of $2,087M has several implications:

1. **Increased external financing need:** The company would need to raise an additional $2,087M in external financing to maintain the same capex program.

2. **Impact on Consolidated Leverage Ratio:** The Consolidated Leverage Ratio is defined as consolidated funded indebtedness (net of 85% of unrestricted cash) to Consolidated EBITDA. A 25% decline in Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA would reduce Consolidated EBITDA, potentially pushing the ratio closer to or above the 3.75x covenant threshold.

3. **Cash burn acceleration:** The company is already in a heavy cash burn phase, with operating cash flow of $1,047M in Q1 2026 vs. capex of $10,107M. A reduction in Connectivity EBITDA would exacerbate this gap.

4. **Reliance on Connectivity as cash engine:** The Connectivity segment is the primary cash-generative segment, contributing $2,087M of the $1,127M total Segment Adjusted EBITDA in Q1 2026 (more than 100% of the total, since Space and AI are negative). A 25% decline would significantly impair the company's ability to internally fund its operations.

5. **Bridge Loan repayment pressure:** The SpaceX Bridge Loan requires repayment from IPO proceeds within 6 months. The company's ability to service this debt depends on its cash generation capacity.

6. **Liquidity buffer:** The company has $15,852M in cash and $7,823M in marketable securities, providing a buffer. However, at the current burn rate, this buffer would be depleted faster.

Let me calculate the Consolidated Leverage Ratio impact more precisely.

Actually, the Consolidated Leverage Ratio uses "Consolidated EBITDA" as defined in the loan agreement, which may differ from Segment Adjusted EBITDA. Let me note this caveat.

Let me also think about the percentage impact:
- The reduction of $2,087M represents a 25% decline in Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA
- This represents a 46.4% decline in total Segment Adjusted EBITDA ($2,087M / $4,508M)
- This represents a 5.2% increase in the funding gap ($2,087M / $40,428M total capex)

Wait, let me recalculate:
- Total Segment Adjusted EBITDA annualized: $4,508M
- Reduction: $2,087M
- Percentage decline in total: $2,087M / $4,508M = 46.3%

So a 25% decline in Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA translates to a 46.3% decline in total Segment Adjusted EBITDA, because Connectivity is the only positive contributor (and more than 100% of the total).

Let me also think about the funding gap:
- Before: Total Segment Adjusted EBITDA - Total Capex = $4,508M - $40,428M = -$35,920M
- After: $2,421M - $40,428M = -$38,007M
- Increase in funding gap: $2,087M

Or focusing on Connectivity vs AI capex:
- Before: $8,348M - $30,892M = -$22,544M
- After: $6,261M - $30,892M = -$24,631M
- Increase in funding gap: $2,087M

The reduction in internal funding capacity is $2,087M (or $2.087 billion).

Let me also calculate the impact on the Consolidated Leverage Ratio. The ratio is:
Consolidated funded indebtedness (net of 85% of unrestricted cash) / Consolidated EBITDA

Current debt: $29,132M
Current cash: $15,852M
85% of unrestricted cash: $15,852M × 0.85 = $13,474.2M
Net debt: $29,132M - $13,474.2M = $15,657.8M

If we use annualized Q1 2026 Segment Adjusted EBITDA as a proxy for Consolidated EBITDA:
Current Consolidated EBITDA (proxy): $4,508M
Current Consolidated Leverage Ratio (proxy): $15,657.8M / $4,508M = 3.47x

After 25% decline in Connectivity:
New Consolidated EBITDA (proxy): $2,421M
New Consolidated Leverage Ratio (proxy): $15,657.8M / $2,421M = 6.47x

This would significantly exceed the 3.75x covenant threshold (and even the 4.25x temporary step-up).

However, I should note that the Consolidated EBITDA as defined in the loan agreement may differ from Segment Adjusted EBITDA. The loan agreement definition may include add-backs or exclusions that aren't captured in Segment Adjusted EBITDA. So this is a rough proxy.

Let me also consider the cash runway impact:
- Current cash + marketable securities: $15,852M + $7,823M = $23,675M
- Current annualized funding gap (all segments): $35,920M
- Cash runway: $23,675M / $35,920M = 0.66 years (about 8 months)

After 25% decline in Connectivity:
- New annualized funding gap: $38,007M
- Cash runway: $23,675M / $38,007M = 0.62 years (about 7.5 months)

So the cash runway would shorten by about 0.5 months.

Wait, this doesn't account for the financing activities. The company has been raising significant external financing. Let me reconsider.

Actually, the question is about "internal funding capacity" which is the cash generated internally (from operations/EBITDA) that can fund capex. The reduction in this capacity means the company needs to raise more external financing.

Let me finalize my analysis:

**Quantification of Reduction in Internal Funding Capacity:**

1. **Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA (annualized Q1 2026):** $8,348M
2. **25% decline:** $2,087M reduction
3. **New Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA:** $6,261M
4. **AI Capex (annualized Q1 2026):** $30,892M
5. **Internal funding capacity (Connectivity vs AI capex):**
   - Before: $8,348M - $30,892M = -$22,544M (deficit)
   - After: $6,261M - $30,892M = -$24,631M (deficit)
   - Reduction: $2,087M

**Implications for Financing Flexibility:**

1. **Increased external financing need:** $2,087M additional external financing required annually
2. **Consolidated Leverage Ratio pressure:** The ratio would increase significantly, potentially breaching the 3.75x covenant
3. **Cash burn acceleration:** The company's already negative free cash flow would worsen
4. **Reliance on Connectivity as cash engine:** Connectivity is the sole positive contributor to Segment Adjusted EBITDA; a 25% decline would reduce total Segment Adjusted EBITDA by 46.3%
5. **Bridge Loan repayment pressure:** The company's ability to service and repay the $20B Bridge Loan depends on its cash generation
6. **Liquidity buffer erosion:** The $23.7B liquidity buffer would be depleted faster

Let me verify my calculations using the calculator tool.

Now let me calculate the impact of the 25% decline:

1. Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA (annualized Q1 2026): $8,348M
2. 25% decline: $8,348M × 0.25 = $2,087M
3. New Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA: $8,348M × 0.75 = $6,261M

Internal funding capacity (Connectivity vs AI capex):
- Before: $8,348M - $30,892M = -$22,544M
- After: $6,261M - $30,892M = -$24,631M
- Reduction: $2,087M

Total Segment Adjusted EBITDA:
- Before: $4,508M
- After: $4,508M - $2,087M = $2,421M
- Percentage decline: $2,087M / $4,508M = 46.3%

Total funding gap (all segments vs total capex):
- Before: $4,508M - $40,428M = -$35,920M
- After: $2,421M - $40,428M = -$38,007M
- Increase in gap: $2,087M

Let me calculate these.

Now let me calculate the Consolidated Leverage Ratio impact:

Current debt: $29,132M
Current cash: $15,852M
85% of unrestricted cash: $15,852M × 0.85 = $13,474.2M
Net debt: $29,132M - $13,474.2M = $15,657.8M

If we use annualized Q1 2026 Segment Adjusted EBITDA as a proxy for Consolidated EBITDA:
Current Consolidated EBITDA (proxy): $4,508M
Current Consolidated Leverage Ratio (proxy): $15,657.8M / $4,508M = 3.47x

After 25% decline in Connectivity:
New Consolidated EBITDA (proxy): $2,421M
New Consolidated Leverage Ratio (proxy): $15,657.8M / $2,421M = 6.47x

Let me calculate these.

Now I have all the calculations I need. Let me also calculate the percentage increase in the funding gap and the cash runway impact.

Cash runway:
- Before: $23,675M / $35,920M = 0.659 years (about 7.9 months)
- After: $23,675M / $38,007M = 0.623 years (about 7.5 months)
- Reduction: 0.036 years (about 0.4 months)

Let me also calculate the percentage increase in the funding gap:
- Before: $35,920M
- After: $38,007M
- Increase: $2,087M
- Percentage increase: $2,087M / $35,920M = 5.81%

And the percentage increase in the Connectivity-only funding gap:
- Before: $22,544M
- After: $24,631M
- Increase: $2,087M
- Percentage increase: $2,087M / $22,544M = 9.26%

Let me calculate these.

Connectivity / Total = $2,087M / $1,127M = 185.2% (Q1 2026)
Or annualized: $8,348M / $4,508M = 185.2%

Now let me compile the final answer.

Summary of key findings:

**Reduction in Internal Funding Capacity: $2,087M ($2.087 billion)**

**Implications for Financing Flexibility:**

1. **Increased external financing need:** $2,087M additional external financing required annually to maintain the same capex program.

2. **Consolidated Leverage Ratio pressure:** 
   - Current (proxy): 3.47x (within 3.75x covenant)
   - After 25% decline (proxy): 6.47x (well above 3.75x covenant, and even above 4.25x temporary step-up)

3. **Cash burn acceleration:**

   - Connectivity-only funding gap increases from $22,544M to $24,631M (9.3% increase)

4. **Reliance on Connectivity as cash engine:**
   - Connectivity represents 185% of total Segment Adjusted EBITDA (because Space and AI are negative)
   - A 25% decline in Connectivity translates to a 46.3% decline in total Segment Adjusted EBITDA
   - This highlights the concentration risk in the Connectivity segment

5. **Cash runway impact:**
   - Current liquidity: $23,675M (cash + marketable securities)
   - Cash runway before: ~7.9 months
   - Cash runway after: ~7.5 months
   - Reduction: ~0.4 months

6. **Bridge Loan repayment pressure:**
   - The $20B SpaceX Bridge Loan requires repayment from IPO proceeds within 6 months
   - The company's ability to service this debt depends on its cash generation capacity
   - A decline in Connectivity EBITDA would impair this capacity

7. **Covenant breach risk:**

Let me now write up the final answer.

Also, I should note that the question asks about "internal funding capacity" which I'm interpreting as the cash generated internally (from operations/EBITDA) that can fund capex. arguments.final_result:
## Analysis: Impact of 25% Decline in Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA on Internal Funding Capacity

### Base-Case Figures (Q1 2026 Annualized)

All figures sourced from the SpaceX S-1 (filed May 20, 2026), which retrospectively recasts consolidated financials to include xAI (merged Feb 2, 2026) and X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025).

| Metric | Q1 2026 (Reported) | Annualized (×4) |
|---|---|---|
| Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA | $2,087M | **$8,348M** |
| AI Segment Capex | $7,723M | **$30,892M** |
| Total Segment Adjusted EBITDA (all segments) | $1,127M | $4,508M |
| Total Capex (all segments) | $10,107M | $40,428M |

Key structural observation: Connectivity is the **sole positive contributor** to Segment Adjusted EBITDA — it represents **185%** of the total (Space = $(351)M and AI = $(609)M in Q1 2026). AI capex represents **76%** of total capex.

---

### Step 1: Apply 25% Decline to Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA

- Annualized Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA (base): $8,348M
- 25% decline: $8,348M × 0.25 = **$2,087M reduction**
- Post-decline annualized Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA: $8,348M × 0.75 = **$6,261M**

### Step 2: Quantify Reduction in Internal Funding Capacity

**Internal funding capacity** = internally generated cash (Segment Adjusted EBITDA) available to fund capex. With AI capex held flat at Q1 2026 annualized levels ($30,892M):

| Metric | Base Case | Stress Case (−25% Connectivity) | Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA | $8,348M | $6,261M | −$2,087M |
| AI Capex (annualized) | $30,892M | $30,892M | $0 |
| **Connectivity-funded AI capex coverage (deficit)** | **$(22,544)M** | **$(24,631)M** | **−$2,087M** |
| Total Segment Adjusted EBITDA | $4,508M | $2,421M | −$2,087M |
| Total Capex (annualized) | $40,428M | $40,428M | $0 |
| **Total internal funding gap** | **$(35,920)M** | **$(38,007)M** | **−$2,087M** |

**Reduction in internal funding capacity: $2,087 million ($2.09 billion) annually.**

Because Connectivity is the only cash-generative segment, a 25% decline in Connectivity EBITDA translates to a **46.3% decline in total Segment Adjusted EBITDA** ($2,087M / $4,508M) — a disproportionate impact reflecting the segment's role as the sole positive contributor.

---

### Step 3: Implications for Financing Flexibility

#### 1. Increased External Financing Requirement
The company must raise an additional **~$2.1B per year** in external financing (debt or equity) to maintain the same AI capex program. This compounds an already large funding gap: the company reported negative "free cash flow" of approximately $(9.1)B in Q1 2026 alone (operating cash flow $1,047M vs. capex $10,107M).

#### 2. Consolidated Leverage Ratio Covenant Pressure
The SpaceX Bridge Loan ($20.0B principal) requires maintaining a Consolidated Leverage Ratio ≤ 3.75x (temporarily 4.25x following a qualifying acquisition). The ratio is defined as consolidated funded indebtedness (net of 85% of unrestricted cash) divided by Consolidated EBITDA.

Using annualized Q1 2026 Segment Adjusted EBITDA as a proxy for Consolidated EBITDA (caveat: the loan agreement's defined "Consolidated EBITDA" may include additional add-backs not captured in Segment Adjusted EBITDA):

| Metric | Base Case | Stress Case |
|---|---|---|
| Total debt (3/31/26) | $29,132M | $29,132M |
| Less: 85% of unrestricted cash ($15,852M) | ($13,474M) | ($13,474M) |
| Net funded indebtedness | $15,658M | $15,658M |
| Consolidated EBITDA (proxy, annualized) | $4,508M | $2,421M |
| **Consolidated Leverage Ratio (proxy)** | **3.47x** | **6.47x** |

A 25% Connectivity decline would push the proxy leverage ratio from **3.47x (compliant)** to **6.47x** — an **86% increase** that would breach both the 3.75x maintenance covenant and the 4.25x temporary step-up. This would risk:
- Default under the $20.0B Bridge Loan
- Loss of access to the $1.5B undrawn revolving credit facility
- Acceleration risk on $29.1B of total debt

#### 3. Cash Runway Compression
As of March 31, 2026, the company held $15,852M cash + $7,823M short-term marketable securities = **$23,675M total liquidity**.

| Metric | Base Case | Stress Case |
|---|---|---|
| Total annualized funding gap | $35,920M | $38,007M |
| Implied cash runway (liquidity / gap) | ~7.9 months | ~7.5 months |

The runway shortens by approximately **0.4 months**, modest in isolation but meaningful given the company's reliance on continuous capital markets access.

#### 4. Heightened Concentration Risk
The analysis underscores that SpaceX's internal funding capacity is **almost entirely dependent on the Connectivity (Starlink) segment**. Connectivity generates 185% of consolidated Segment Adjusted EBITDA (Space and AI are both negative). A 25% adverse move in this single segment eliminates nearly half of the company's total internal cash generation, demonstrating extreme sensitivity to Starlink economics.

#### 5. Bridge Loan Repayment Dynamics
The SpaceX Bridge Loan matures September 2, 2027 (extendable to March 2, 2028), and the company is **required to apply net IPO proceeds to repay the Bridge Loan within six months of receipt**. A weaker Connectivity profile could:
- Pressure IPO valuation and proceeds available for repayment
- Increase refinancing risk on the $20.0B Bridge Loan
- Tighten the maturity wall (no principal payments currently due until August 2027)

#### 6. Offset: Stated Capex Flexibility
The filing explicitly notes management retains flexibility to adjust AI capex: *"If near-term data center needs decrease or ramp more slowly than expected, [the company] can reduce capital expenditures in that segment and reallocate funds to other business priorities."* This provides a partial mitigant — the company could slow AI capex to preserve liquidity — but doing so would compromise the AI growth thesis underpinning the post-merger valuation.

---

### Summary

| Item | Value |
|---|---|
| **Reduction in internal funding capacity (annual)** | **$2,087M ($2.09B)** |
| Equivalent decline in total Segment Adjusted EBITDA | 46.3% |
| Increase in total annual funding gap | $2,087M (from $35.9B to $38.0B) |
| Proxy Consolidated Leverage Ratio shift | 3.47x → 6.47x (breaches 3.75x covenant) |
| Additional external financing required annually | ~$2.1B |
| Cash runway compression | ~7.9 → ~7.5 months |

**Conclusion:** A 25% decline in Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA, with AI capex held at Q1 2026 annualized levels ($30.9B), would reduce internal funding capacity by approximately **$2.09 billion annually**. Because Connectivity is the sole positive EBITDA contributor, this decline disproportionately impairs total internal cash generation (−46.3%) and would likely breach the Consolidated Leverage Ratio covenant on the $20.0B SpaceX Bridge Loan, materially constraining financing flexibility and elevating refinancing risk on $29.1B of total debt. The company's primary mitigants are its $23.7B liquidity buffer and stated ability to modulate AI capex, but both come at the cost of growth-optionality and IPO-valuation support.

---

### Sources

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "SpaceX S-1 Registration Statement (filed May 20, 2026) — Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation, capital expenditures by segment, liquidity and capital resources, SpaceX Bridge Loan terms, Consolidated Leverage Ratio covenant"
    }
  ]
}

All figures sourced from the SpaceX S-1 (filed May 20, 2026), which retrospectively recasts consolidated financials to include xAI (merged Feb 2, 2026) and X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025).

| Metric | Q1 2026 (Reported) | Annualized (×4) |
|---|---|---|
| Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA | $2,087M | **$8,348M** |
| AI Segment Capex | $7,723M | **$30,892M** |
| Total Segment Adjusted EBITDA (all segments) | $1,127M | $4,508M |
| Total Capex (all segments) | $10,107M | $40,428M |

Key structural observation: Connectivity is the **sole positive contributor** to Segment Adjusted EBITDA — it represents **185%** of the total (Space = $(351)M and AI = $(609)M in Q1 2026). AI capex represents **76%** of total capex.

---

### Step 1: Apply 25% Decline to Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA

- Annualized Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA (base): $8,348M
- 25% decline: $8,348M × 0.25 = **$2,087M reduction**
- Post-decline annualized Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA: $8,348M × 0.75 = **$6,261M**

### Step 2: Quantify Reduction in Internal Funding Capacity

**Internal funding capacity** = internally generated cash (Segment Adjusted EBITDA) available to fund capex. With AI capex held flat at Q1 2026 annualized levels ($30,892M):

| Metric | Base Case | Stress Case (−25% Connectivity) | Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA | $8,348M | $6,261M | −$2,087M |
| AI Capex (annualized) | $30,892M | $30,892M | $0 |
| **Connectivity-funded AI capex coverage (deficit)** | **$(22,544)M** | **$(24,631)M** | **−$2,087M** |
| Total Segment Adjusted EBITDA | $4,508M | $2,421M | −$2,087M |
| Total Capex (annualized) | $40,428M | $40,428M | $0 |
| **Total internal funding gap** | **$(35,920)M** | **$(38,007)M** | **−$2,087M** |

**Reduction in internal funding capacity: $2,087 million ($2.09 billion) annually.**

Because Connectivity is the only cash-generative segment, a 25% decline in Connectivity EBITDA translates to a **46.3% decline in total Segment Adjusted EBITDA** ($2,087M / $4,508M) — a disproportionate impact reflecting the segment's role as the sole positive contributor.

---

### Step 3: Implications for Financing Flexibility

#### 1. Increased External Financing Requirement
The company must raise an additional **~$2.1B per year** in external financing (debt or equity) to maintain the same AI capex program. This compounds an already large funding gap: the company reported negative "free cash flow" of approximately $(9.1)B in Q1 2026 alone (operating cash flow $1,047M vs. capex $10,107M).

#### 2. Consolidated Leverage Ratio Covenant Pressure
The SpaceX Bridge Loan ($20.0B principal) requires maintaining a Consolidated Leverage Ratio ≤ 3.75x (temporarily 4.25x following a qualifying acquisition). The ratio is defined as consolidated funded indebtedness (net of 85% of unrestricted cash) divided by Consolidated EBITDA.

Using annualized Q1 2026 Segment Adjusted EBITDA as a proxy for Consolidated EBITDA (caveat: the loan agreement's defined "Consolidated EBITDA" may include additional add-backs not captured in Segment Adjusted EBITDA):

| Metric | Base Case | Stress Case |
|---|---|---|
| Total debt (3/31/26) | $29,132M | $29,132M |
| Less: 85% of unrestricted cash ($15,852M) | ($13,474M) | ($13,474M) |
| Net funded indebtedness | $15,658M | $15,658M |
| Consolidated EBITDA (proxy, annualized) | $4,508M | $2,421M |
| **Consolidated Leverage Ratio (proxy)** | **3.47x** | **6.47x** |

A 25% Connectivity decline would push the proxy leverage ratio from **3.47x (compliant)** to **6.47x** — an **86% increase** that would breach both the 3.75x maintenance covenant and the 4.25x temporary step-up. This would risk:
- Default under the $20.0B Bridge Loan
- Loss of access to the $1.5B undrawn revolving credit facility
- Acceleration risk on $29.1B of total debt

#### 3. Cash Runway Compression
As of March 31, 2026, the company held $15,852M cash + $7,823M short-term marketable securities = **$23,675M total liquidity**.

| Metric | Base Case | Stress Case |
|---|---|---|
| Total annualized funding gap | $35,920M | $38,007M |
| Implied cash runway (liquidity / gap) | ~7.9 months | ~7.5 months |

The runway shortens by approximately **0.4 months**, modest in isolation but meaningful given the company's reliance on continuous capital markets access.

#### 4. Heightened Concentration Risk
The analysis underscores that SpaceX's internal funding capacity is **almost entirely dependent on the Connectivity (Starlink) segment**. Connectivity generates 185% of consolidated Segment Adjusted EBITDA (Space and AI are both negative). A 25% adverse move in this single segment eliminates nearly half of the company's total internal cash generation, demonstrating extreme sensitivity to Starlink economics.

#### 5. Bridge Loan Repayment Dynamics
The SpaceX Bridge Loan matures September 2, 2027 (extendable to March 2, 2028), and the company is **required to apply net IPO proceeds to repay the Bridge Loan within six months of receipt**. A weaker Connectivity profile could:
- Pressure IPO valuation and proceeds available for repayment
- Increase refinancing risk on the $20.0B Bridge Loan
- Tighten the maturity wall (no principal payments currently due until August 2027)

#### 6. Offset: Stated Capex Flexibility
The filing explicitly notes management retains flexibility to adjust AI capex: *"If near-term data center needs decrease or ramp more slowly than expected, [the company] can reduce capital expenditures in that segment and reallocate funds to other business priorities."* This provides a partial mitigant — the company could slow AI capex to preserve liquidity — but doing so would compromise the AI growth thesis underpinning the post-merger valuation.

---

### Summary

| Item | Value |
|---|---|
| **Reduction in internal funding capacity (annual)** | **$2,087M ($2.09B)** |
| Equivalent decline in total Segment Adjusted EBITDA | 46.3% |
| Increase in total annual funding gap | $2,087M (from $35.9B to $38.0B) |
| Proxy Consolidated Leverage Ratio shift | 3.47x → 6.47x (breaches 3.75x covenant) |
| Additional external financing required annually | ~$2.1B |
| Cash runway compression | ~7.9 → ~7.5 months |

**Conclusion:** A 25% decline in Connectivity Segment Adjusted EBITDA, with AI capex held at Q1 2026 annualized levels ($30.9B), would reduce internal funding capacity by approximately **$2.09 billion annually**. Because Connectivity is the sole positive EBITDA contributor, this decline disproportionately impairs total internal cash generation (−46.3%) and would likely breach the Consolidated Leverage Ratio covenant on the $20.0B SpaceX Bridge Loan, materially constraining financing flexibility and elevating refinancing risk on $29.1B of total debt. The company's primary mitigants are its $23.7B liquidity buffer and stated ability to modulate AI capex, but both come at the cost of growth-optionality and IPO-valuation support.
